title: Workbook — Moral Conscience
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class, at home, or in a group.

I. Reviewing the Essentials

  1. What is the Catechism’s definition of conscience in CCC 1776?
  2. What three moments of the judgment of conscience exist (before, during, after)? Explain them in your own words.
  3. What are the five principal means for forming conscience?
  4. What is the difference between vincible ignorance and invincible ignorance?
  5. Why is conscience not autonomous? What does it mean that it is at the service of truth?
  6. What does the principle “never do evil that good may come of it” mean? Give an example.

II. For Reflection and Writing

1. Describe a situation in which your conscience clearly told you that something was evil, even though other people were encouraging you to do it. Where do you think that inner voice came from? What did you finally do?

 

2. Examine your conscience right now. Is there something you have been ignoring for a long time, something you know is wrong but you keep doing? Why do you think you have not changed it?

 

3. How would you distinguish between what your conscience tells you, what your feelings tell you, and what social pressure tells you? What criteria would you use?

 

4. Imagine a friend says to you: “I follow my conscience, and my conscience tells me this is not wrong.” Using what you have learned, what would you ask to help him discern?

 

5. Of the witnesses presented (Newman, Thomas More, Bonhoeffer, the monks of Tibhirine), which one impacted you most and why?

 

III. Guided Examination of Conscience

Use this guide to make a personal examination of conscience. Answer with sincerity before God, without rushing. You can use it to prepare for your next Confession.

My relationship with God

  • Do I pray every day, or only when I need something?
  • Have I missed Mass on any Sunday or Holy Day of Obligation out of laziness?
  • Have I gone to confession this year? Have I concealed any grave sin in confession?
  • Have I received Communion while not in the state of grace?
  • Have I doubted the faith or God’s mercy?
  • Have I resorted to superstitions, horoscopes, or occult practices?

My relationship with others

  • Have I disrespected my parents or those who have authority over me?
  • Have I lied, slandered, or spoken badly of others behind their backs?
  • Have I stolen anything, however small? Have I returned what was not mine?
  • Have I held grudges or refused to forgive someone?
  • Have I been selfish with my time, money, or attention?
  • Have I helped those who needed it, or have I looked the other way?

My relationship with myself

  • Have I voluntarily consented to impure thoughts?
  • Have I consumed pornography or inappropriate content?
  • Have I spoken or joked in an obscene or vulgar manner?
  • Have I eaten or drunk to excess?
  • Have I been lazy in my duties —studies, work, family?
  • Have I been proud, vain, or envious of others?
  • Have I become discouraged and given up in the spiritual struggle?

My life in truth

  • Have I been sincere with myself, or have I deceived myself?
  • Is there any vice or attachment that is dominating my life?
  • What is it that I am most ashamed to acknowledge? —that is the key point.
  • Is there something I know I must change but am not changing?

After the examination, pray an Act of Contrition. If you have discovered grave sins, go to Confession as soon as possible.

IV. Practical Activities

  1. The Ignatian Examen: Practice the five steps of the daily examen (give thanks, ask for light, examine, ask for forgiveness, resolution) throughout the week. Write one line each day in a notebook about what you discovered.
  2. Read the Catechism: Read CCC 1776-1802 slowly. Underline two ideas that surprise or challenge you. Share them with someone.
  3. Psalm 139: Pray Psalm 139:23-24 every morning this week. At the end of the week, write down what God has revealed to you about your conscience.
  4. Confession: If you have detected grave sins or it has been more than a month since your last confession, find a church and go this week. Use the guided examination from Section III.
  5. A witness: Research a little more about the life of one of the witnesses presented (Newman, Thomas More, Bonhoeffer, the monks of Tibhirine). Write down what it teaches you about following conscience.
  6. Share the faith: Explain to someone what you have learned in this class. It is not about giving a sermon, but about sharing something that has helped you.

V. Memory Verse

The aim of our charge is love that issues from a pure heart and a good conscience and sincere faith.

— 1 Timothy 1:5

VI. Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus, you know my heart better than I know myself. Search me and reveal where my conscience has been numbed, where I have ignored your voice, where I have rationalized my sins. Give me the courage to face what you reveal, and the humility to change. Form my conscience by your Word, by the teaching of your Church, and by the light of your Spirit. Give me the strength of St. John Henry Newman, who followed his conscience even when it cost him everything. Give me the integrity of St. Thomas More, who was faithful to the truth unto death. Make me quick to recognize evil, and swift to choose good. May my conscience be clean, my heart pure, and my faith sincere. Amen.

St. Thomas More, pray for us.
St. John Henry Newman, pray for us.
Holy Mary, mirror of justice, pray for us.

Print Workbook
